common_name: Mexican Bay Leaf (Mexican Avocado Leaf, Hoja de Aguacate)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Mexican bay leaf is an avocado-family tree native to Mexico and Central America whose dried or fresh leaves impart a distinctive anise-cinnamon-sassafras flavor — an essential ingredient in Oaxacan mole negro, mole coloradito, and other southern Mexican dishes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; harvest leaves as needed throughout the growing season and dry for storage. Persea drymifolia (Mexican bay leaf or hoja de aguacate) is a Mexican and Central American tree in the avocado family (Lauraceae) grown primarily for its strongly aromatic leaves, which have a complex, distinctive fragrance and flavor combining anise (star anise), cinnamon, and sassafras. The leaves are not related to European bay laurel (Laurus nobilis) but occupy a similar culinary role in southern Mexican cooking — they are an essential, non-substitutable ingredient in Oaxacan mole negro, tasajo, estofado, and tamales, where they are toasted on a comal (griddle) before use to intensify their flavor and then added whole to slow-cooked dishes. The trees also occasionally produce small, avocado-like fruits that are inedible but closely resemble tiny avocados. In zone 9–11, the tree grows as a small to medium ornamental specimen; the leaves can be dried and stored for use. Dried Mexican bay leaves can be purchased at Mexican grocery stores but fresh-from-the-tree leaves are far more fragrant. A unique culinary herb tree that no serious Oaxacan cooking enthusiast should be without.
sources: Diana Kennedy — The Cuisines of Mexico
USDA GRIN Taxonomy — Persea drymifolia
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Bolivian coriander is a fast-growing, strongly scented South American herb with an aroma and flavor combining cilantro, rue, and black pepper — it is used fresh as a cilantro substitute in Bolivian, Peruvian, and southern Mexican cuisine, especially in salsas and ceviches.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in lean, well-drained soil in full sun; harvest tender young leaves frequently. Porophyllum ruderale (Bolivian coriander or quilquiña) is a fast-growing, erect annual herb from South America in the daisy family (Asteraceae) — notable for the distinctive oil glands visible as dark dots on the leaves when held to light. The leaves have an intensely aromatic flavor that is often described as combining cilantro (coriander) with rue and black pepper — pungent, citrus-herbal, with a slightly sharp, medicinal undertone. In Bolivia, the herb is called 'quilquiña' and is used fresh (never cooked — heat destroys the volatile oils) as a condiment for salteñas, silpancho, and sauces; in Peru and Colombia it is called 'papalo quelite' and used in ceviches, guacamole, and fresh salsas. The herb is especially valued in regions where cilantro bolts rapidly in summer heat — Porophyllum is more heat-tolerant and continues producing leaves through summer when cilantro fails. A closely related species, P. macrocephalum (also called papalo), is grown in Mexico and the southwestern US. The leaves must be used fresh and sparingly — the flavor is roughly 4-5 times more intense than cilantro.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Porophyllum ruderale
Universidad Mayor de San Andres (Bolivia) — Herbal Plants
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Bolivian Coriander

common_name: Spanish Thyme (Jamaican Oregano, Puerto Rican Oregano)

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Spanish thyme is a small Caribbean shrub with intensely thyme-oregano scented leaves — it is the traditional dry-climate oregano and thyme substitute of Jamaica and the Caribbean islands, far more drought-tolerant than either European thyme or oregano.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; harvest stems frequently to maintain compact, bushy growth. Lippia micromera (Spanish thyme or Jamaican oregano) is a small, woody, aromatic shrub native to the dry limestone hills of Jamaica and other Caribbean islands, producing small, densely crowded, grey-green, strongly thyme-oregano scented leaves. The plant occupies the culinary niche of thyme and oregano in Caribbean cooking — the dried or fresh leaves are used in jerk seasoning blends, seasoning rubs for meats and fish, and traditional Caribbean herbal teas (sooshal, orosul). It is notably more drought- and heat-tolerant than either European thyme (Thymus vulgaris) or Mediterranean oregano (Origanum vulgare), making it well-suited to hot, dry, tropical and subtropical gardens where those herbs struggle. Lippia micromera belongs to the verbena family (Verbenaceae) and is distinct from Plectranthus amboinicus (Cuban oregano, also sometimes called 'Spanish thyme') — both are Caribbean oregano substitutes but from unrelated plant families. The essential oil is rich in thymol and carvacrol, the same primary compounds responsible for thyme and oregano's characteristic aroma. Excellent in containers in subtropical gardens.
sources: University of the West Indies — Caribbean Medicinal Herbs
Trinidad and Tobago Ministry of Agriculture — Herbs
USDA ARS — Germplasm Resources Information Network (GRIN)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), fertile soil in full shade to part shade; dog hobble is a native Appalachian understory shrub with long, arching branches and glossy, evergreen foliage that turns attractive burgundy-bronze in winter — one of the finest native shade shrubs for woodland gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil in deep to part shade; mulch with pine bark or pine needles to maintain soil acidity. Leucothoe fontanesiana (dog hobble or drooping leucothoe) is an elegant native Appalachian understory shrub growing along shaded stream banks and moist forest floors from Virginia south to Georgia and Tennessee. The plant produces long (3–5 foot), gracefully arching stems clothed in glossy, lance-shaped, evergreen leaves — in summer a rich, dark green, transforming to dramatic shades of burgundy, bronze, and red-purple in winter (especially in cultivars like 'Rainbow' and 'Girard's Rainbow'). In late spring, drooping clusters of urn-shaped, white flowers (resembling lily-of-the-valley flowers) line the undersides of the arching branches — fragrant and attractive. Dog hobble earns its common name from the impenetrable tangles it forms along mountain creek banks, through which hunting dogs cannot easily pass. It spreads slowly by rhizomes to form colonies. As a shade garden plant, Leucothoe fontanesiana provides outstanding year-round interest: spring flowers, summer foliage, and winter color in sites too acidic and shady for most other shrubs. The cultivar 'Scarletta' has especially vivid scarlet winter color.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Native Shrubs
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
American Horticultural Society — Shade Gardening

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), fertile soil in part shade; miracle berry is a West African rain forest understory shrub that requires acidic, humus-rich, consistently moist conditions similar to blueberries — it cannot tolerate alkaline water or soil and performs best in acidic, non-chlorinated water.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil in part shade; water with rainwater or filtered water — chlorinated tap water and alkaline water damage the plant. Synsepalum dulcificum (miracle berry or miracle fruit) is a West African evergreen shrub that produces the most remarkable edible berry in the botanical world: small (1-inch), red, olive-shaped berries containing a glycoprotein called miraculin, which temporarily binds to sour taste receptors on the tongue and causes sour, acidic foods to taste intensely sweet for 30–60 minutes after eating a single berry. The berries themselves have virtually no sugar content and a bland flavor — the effect is entirely on taste perception, not on the food itself. After eating a miracle berry, lemons taste like lemon candy, vinegar tastes sweet, sour cream becomes sweet cream, and Tabasco sauce tastes like hot doughnut glaze. Miracle berry parties (where participants eat the berry and sample unusual flavor combinations) have become a culinary novelty. The plant is native to West African rain forest understory and requires the same acidic, humus-rich, moist, part-shade conditions as blueberries — it is extremely pH-sensitive and will decline rapidly in alkaline soil or water. In zone 10–12, it can be grown outdoors; in zone 8–9, best in a pot brought indoors in winter.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Miracle Fruit
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Synsepalum dulcificum
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Unusual Tropical Fruits

planting.note: Plant container stock or fresh seed in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; gandaria is a Thai and Southeast Asian tropical fruit tree in the mango family (Anacardiaceae) that produces oval, mango-scented fruits ranging from tart-green to sweet-golden at full ripeness — eaten fresh, in Thai somtam salads, and as a souring agent in curries.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; gandaria prefers consistent moisture and excellent drainage. Bouea macrophylla (gandaria or Marian plum) is a Thai, Malay, and Indonesian tropical fruit tree in the mango family (Anacardiaceae) — closely related to mango (Mangifera) and producing small (1–1.5 inch), oval, egg-shaped fruits with a thin, smooth skin progressing from bright green through yellow-orange to golden when fully ripe, surrounding a large seed. The fruit's flavor profile is remarkably mango-like — fragrant, sweet-tart, resinous — but in miniature form; the flesh is juicy, fibrous, and intensely aromatic. Unripe fruits are highly acidic (like green mango) and are used as a souring agent in Thai and Malay curries, fish dishes, and the Thai-Malay salad-style dish 'rujak.' Ripe fruits are sweet enough to eat fresh. The tree is dense, compact compared to true mango, with large, glossy, dark-green leaves and clusters of small cream flowers. Gandaria is extremely popular in rural and home gardens of Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand but is almost completely unknown in Western horticulture. For tropical zone 11–12 gardeners seeking unusual, genuine Southeast Asian fruits.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Bouea macrophylla
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Plants of the World Online
Thai Department of Agriculture — Tropical Fruits

common_name: Water Apple (Watery Rose Apple, Bell Fruit)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to periodically waterlogged, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; water apple is a Southeast Asian tropical fruit tree that tolerates wet soils and periodic flooding and produces crisp, bell-shaped, mildly sweet, extremely watery fruits — very distinct from the rose apple (Syzygium jambos) in fruit size, texture, and tree form.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; water apple tolerates periodic waterlogging and is well-suited to wet, low-lying areas. Syzygium aqueum (water apple or watery rose apple) is a Southeast Asian tropical fruit tree native to the Malay Peninsula and Indonesia that produces the most 'watery' of all the rose apple relatives — distinctive, bell-shaped to pear-shaped fruits (1.5–2 inches long) in white, pale pink, or red, with an extremely high water content (as the name suggests), a thin, glossy skin, crisp texture, and a very mild, barely-sweet, cucumber-like flavor. The fruit is refreshing rather than richly flavored — in tropical heat, the high water content makes water apple a thirst-quenching fruit eaten fresh as a hydrating snack, cut into slices and served with salt and chili, or made into a light agua fresca. The tree is notably tolerant of wet soils and periodic flooding compared to other Syzygium species — it grows naturally along stream banks and in seasonal swamps in its native habitat. It is distinctly different from the rose apple (S. jambos) in its smaller, lighter, more watery fruit, smaller tree size, and tolerance of wet conditions. Widely grown in home gardens throughout Southeast Asia and the Philippines.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Syzygium aqueum
Philippine Department of Agriculture — Tropical Fruits
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rose Apple Relatives

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; climbing ylang ylang is a tropical Indian and Southeast Asian climbing shrub in the custard apple family with intensely fragrant, yellow-green flowers with the characteristic ylang ylang perfume — the scent is extraordinarily powerful at night and the flowers are used in traditional Indian garland-making.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; provide a strong trellis or allow to scramble through a supporting tree. Artabotrys hexapetalus (climbing ylang ylang or manorangini) is a tropical Indian and Southeast Asian climbing shrub in the custard apple family (Annonaceae) that climbs by means of distinctive hooked, curved peduncles (flower stalks) that become woody and hook-like as the flower develops — anchoring the plant to supporting branches without true tendrils or twining. The plant produces small, pale yellow-green to yellow flowers in clusters, with six tepals (three outer, three inner) — the flowers have an extraordinarily powerful, sweet, heady fragrance almost identical to true ylang ylang (Cananga odorata) — intensely sweet, floral, slightly fruity — and are among the most powerfully fragrant flowers in the tropical garden. The scent intensifies dramatically at night, making this plant outstanding for outdoor seating areas in the tropics. In South India, the flowers are used in temple garlands, hair decorations, and in traditional perfume making — the essential oil extracted from the flowers is used in Ayurvedic preparations and in the perfume industry as a low-cost ylang ylang substitute. A remarkable and underused tropical fragrance plant for zone 10–12.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
Indian Council of Agricultural Research
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Tropical Fragrance Plants
